topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white on faded parchment, lay crumpled on my desk. I didn't bother unfolding it completely; the sheer finality of the words was enough. "Effective Spring Semester," it read, followed by a list of departments slated for closure. I stared at the peeling paint on the windowsill, the afternoon sun doing nothing to warm the cold knot tightening in my chest. I picked up my worn copy of *Moby Dick*, the weight of the book grounding me slightly, but it didn't stop the simmering irritation that had been building for months. Why now? After all the late nights fueled by cheap coffee, the endless group projects, the feeling that every sacrifice had been an investment in something that was now being systematically dismantled. I slammed the book shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the suddenly cavernous silence of my apartment. I started pacing, a restless energy thrumming beneath my skin, needing to move, needing to do *something* other than just sit here and let the disappointment settle like dust.

[story 2]

"So, that’s it then?" I asked, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. My voice came out flatter than I intended, a practiced monotone that masked the tremor running through my hands. I leaned back in my chair, letting the cheap vinyl squeak in protest, and looked around the lecture hall. Empty seats. Ghosts of previous semesters lingered in the fluorescent glare. I traced the grain of the wooden desk with a fingernail, the friction a small, sharp distraction from the heavy feeling in my stomach. It wasn't sadness, exactly; it was a profound sense of being cheated. All those hours spent mastering calculus, agonizing over thesis proposals, pouring my entire identity into these brick and mortar walls—and it was all just... disposable. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against linoleum sounding aggressive. I needed to walk out, to feel the grit of the outside world under my shoes, anything to escape the suffocating atmosphere of impending erasure.

[story 3]

I kept re-reading the departmental restructuring memo, my eyes skipping over the jargon, focusing instead on the dates. Another year. Another cycle of administrative decisions overriding actual human effort. A slow burn started in my jaw, a tight clench that made my teeth ache. I walked down to the quad, the autumn air biting at my exposed skin. People were milling about, laughing, heading toward campus events—a vibrant, oblivious energy that felt deliberately exclusionary. I watched them, feeling an increasing distance from their easy camaraderie. I stopped by the old library entrance, running a hand over the cold stone. I felt a distinct urge to argue, to point out the absurdity of the entire situation, to make them see the sheer waste of what we’d built here. But the words caught in my throat, replaced by a dull, heavy resignation. I just stood there, shoulders slumped, letting the silence of the impending closure press down on me like a physical weight.
